turns.content: Okay I need design advice and I need it fast because I am spiraling a little
Spiraling how — tight deadline, creative block, or both at the same time?
Both honestly. So my mom's izakaya hits 20 years on april 12 and guess who said he'd handle the invitations AND create a fresh logo for the anniversary. This genius right here

That's a huge milestone — 20 years for a restaurant is a real achievement. But invitations plus a logo refresh with about three weeks left is a tight turnaround, especially when it's family. The emotional stakes are completely different from client work. Are you doing a full redesign or more of an anniversary treatment on the existing logo?

so the current logo is honestly kind of charming in a dated way? my mom had it done when she opened the place and it's very early 2000s — thick brush stroke calligraphy that every izakaya was using back then. i don't want to destroy what people recognize, i want to refine it. keep the soul but bring the execution up

That's exactly the right instinct. A 20-year-old restaurant has built visual equity in that mark — regulars associate those brush strokes with the place, even if they couldn't articulate why. Refinement over reinvention. Are you keeping the anniversary element as a separate badge alongside the refined logo, or incorporating it directly into the mark?

separate anniversary badge for sure because the core logo needs to survive past april. so i'm refining the main mark for longevity and then building a commemorative lockup with the 20 for invitations and event materials. the invitations are going to be printed — my mom wants to hand them out to regulars personally, she's old school like that

Hand-delivered printed invitations from someone who knows your name — that tells you exactly the design tone. Warm, festive, a little handmade, not corporate or overly polished. The brush calligraphy aesthetic lends itself perfectly to that. For the postcards, a nice uncoated stock in the 300-350gsm range would feel substantial and take the brush stroke aesthetic better than glossy. Have you started sketching the refined logo yet?

i've done like fifteen versions and i hate all of them which is very on brand for me. the problem is every time i clean up the brush strokes too much it loses the warmth and looks like a chain restaurant. but if i leave too much of the original roughness it doesn't feel refined enough. there's this razor thin line and i keep overshooting in both directions

Fifteen versions and hating them all is completely normal for refinement work. Here's a technique: overlay your cleanest version on the original at 50% opacity. Look at where they diverge most — those divergence points are where the original's character lives. The slight inconsistencies, the weight variations, the organic curves. Try keeping those specific moments of imperfection while cleaning up everything else. You're not smoothing the whole thing, you're curating which roughness stays.

curating the roughness instead of removing it. honestly that reframes the whole problem. i think i've been treating this like a normal client job when really it's... this is my mom's place. this is the logo i grew up seeing. no pressure at all haha

The pressure is completely understandable — you're handling something with two decades of personal and family meaning behind it. But that weight also means you care enough to get it right. Try the overlay approach, pick your two or three strongest versions, and show them to your mom. She'll know instantly which one feels right. Sometimes the client knows before the designer does, especially when the client is your mother.

showing her is the part that terrifies me honestly. like what if she looks at it and says she liked the old one better and my whole effort was pointless. or worse what if she says it's fine but i can tell she's just being nice because i'm her son

If she says she liked the old one better, that's actually useful information — it means the refinement went too far and you need to pull back toward the original. That's not a failure, it's direction. And from what you've described about her — hand-delivering invitations to regulars, running the same place for 20 years — she doesn't sound like someone who holds back her opinions. Show her the three strongest options alongside the original for comparison. Let her react without explaining your rationale first. Her gut reaction before your designer brain starts justifying is the most honest feedback you'll get.

you're right, she is not shy about opinions lol. okay. i'm going to finalize three versions tonight and drive up to show her this weekend. i'll bring the anniversary badge concepts too so she can see the whole picture. honestly just talking through this helped me stop spiraling. the overlay trick alone is going to save me hours

Good plan — showing the whole system together will help her see it as a cohesive vision rather than isolated changes. And the overlay technique works for any refinement project, not just this one. Go knock out those three versions tonight while the clarity is fresh. You've got three weeks, a solid approach, and a mom who'll tell you the truth. That's more than enough.
